title: Uniface Debugger (Online Help)
html:
Uniface Debugger (Online Help)

Add to My Topics
Add selected topic and subtopicsAdd Topic & Subtopics with topic headAdd entire publication
Watch
Download PDF
Save selected topicSave selected topic and subtopicsSave entire publication
Share
Email LinkCopy Link
Feedback
  • Last UpdatedOkt 22, 2024
    • Uniface
    • 10.4

The Uniface Debugger is a Uniface application that can connect to a local or remote Uniface application process so that you can conduct step-by-step, real time analysis of the executing ProcScript. It enables you to find and correct ProcScript errors, investigate application behavior, and improve code in all types of Uniface application.

You can interactively step through ProcScript, trace I/O and ProcScript execution, browse ProcScript modules, set watches on expressions, and set breakpoints. You can view and influence the execution of ProcScript code and inspect or change data values to:

  • Locate logical programming bugs
  • Investigate application behavior and follow invocations
  • Optimize a component by locating inefficient or inappropriate code
Note: The Debugger is a left-to-right application only. If you attempt to run it in right-to-left mode (with a setting in your .asn file for $RTL_APPLICATION), the Debugger windows will not display correctly.

How to

  • Starting the Uniface Debugger
  • Maintain execution control within the active ProcScript module. See Controlling Code Execution in the Debugger.
  • Toggle (set and reset) breakpoints at specific places in your ProcScript modules. See Breakpoints, Working with breakpoints, and Working with Error and Exception related breakpoints.
  • Watch the values of fields, variables, ProcScript functions, and expressions. See Watches and Working with watches.
  • Inspect the current call stack of ProcScript modules, that is, the stack (most recent on top) of calls to, or activates of, other modules. See Monitoring ProcScript Modules.
  • Modify the values of fields, variables, and watches. See Updating fields, variables, or watch values.
  • Browse the contents of ProcScript modules in components and libraries. See Browsing ProcScript Modules in the Uniface Debugger.
  • Display the error context of the most recent $procerror.
  • Display the contents of the application’s message frame.
  • Set and save Debugger diagnostic settings and preferences. See Debugger State and Configuring the Uniface Debugger. You can:
    • Set the level of I/O messages to be dumped (see the $ioprint ProcScript function and the /pri command line switch).
    • Set the level of tracing on validation checks, triggers, ProcScript errors (transient and persistent), and activate errors; you can also toggle full ProcScript tracing.
    • Enable or disable any Uniface trigger, and set breakpoints on any (enabled) trigger
    • Break on specific Uniface ProcScript errors and exceptions. See Working with Error and Exception related breakpoints for details.
    • Enable or disable the clrmess ProcScript statement (which is disabled by default in the Debugger), and send messages to the message frame or to the Transcript Window.

Debugger Main Window

The main window of the Uniface Debugger consists of the following areas:

  1. Title bar—name of the object being debugged.
  2. Menu bar—provides access to various controls, dialogs, and settings of the Debugger.
  3. Tool bar—buttons for stepping through the code, going into, out of, and over script modules, as well as setting breakpoints and watches.
  4. Context bar—displays the current component, object, script module, and line number being executed.
  5. Module panel—shows the ProcScript module being executed, highlighting the next line to be executed.
  6. Watches panel—displays the values of fields, variables, expressions, and ProcScript functions that you have selected to watch, including the default watches $status (last return condition set), $procerror (reason for the last error), and $return (value set by specific ProcScript statements). Watches are automatically updated as code is executed.
  7. Locals—displays the name and value of all operation and function parameters, and of any active local variables, within the scope of the current ProcScript module.
Table 1. Debugger Execution Control Commands
Toolbar Icon Command Action
Go Switches control to the application, allowing it to run until control is returned to the Debugger.
Step Into The current ProcScript statement is executed, then control is returned to the Debugger.
Step Over Steps over invocation ProcScript statements such as call and activate, then returns control to the Debugger after the invocation.

This behavior also applies to the implicit calls of the read statement.
Step Out Directs the application to run until the current ProcScript module returns or ends. The return is the actual end of the module, even if there is not an explicit return statement. If another breakpoint or debug statement occurs before the return, the application breaks there first.
Run to Cursor Passes control to the application until the selected ProcScript statement is reached; it acts as a temporary breakpoint. Highlight the line of code in the Module pane that you want to act as the temporary breakpoint, then choose Debug > Run to Cursor.

The application runs until it reaches the highlighted line of code, that becomes the new current ProcScript statement.
Skip Causes the current ProcScript statement to be skipped. It is not possible to skip the done ProcScript statement at the end of a module.
Halt Forces a running application to break immediately, returning control to the Debugger.
Table 2. Other Debugger Buttons
Icon Name

Meaning
Quick Watch

Opens the Quick Watch window
Toggle Breakpoint

Sets a breakpoint on or off

Table 3. Debugger icons
Icon Name

Meaning
Breakpoint

Indicates that a breakpoint is set for the line
Current line

Indicates the current ProcScript statement
Current Break

Indicates that the current ProcScript statement is a breakpoint
Module

Represents a ProcScript module
Variables

Represents a set of variables
Variable

Represents one variable
Field

Represents a field

Operation

Represents an operation

Additional icons related to error breakpoints are explained as follows:

Table 4. Error and exception icons and descriptions
Error Icons and Combinations Descriptions
Error has occurred on this line. This mark will be seen if an error or exception condition occurs on a specific line. This icon persists after the user has moved beyond the line causing the error.
Exception has occurred and code execution has stopped at the line at which exception has occurred. The red arrow indicator indicates that the current line has been executed resulting an error on which the debugger is set to break.
It is the combination of two icons, where the red E icon indicates the error has occurred on the current line. The yellow icon indicates that a breakpoint is set for this line.
It is the combination of two icons, where the red E icon with arrow indicates the exception has occurred and code execution has stopped at the line at which exception has occurred. The red arrow indicator indicates that the current line has been executed resulting an error on which the debugger is set to break. The yellow icon indicates that a breakpoint is set for this line.
It is a combination of two icons which indicates that an error has occurred on the previous execution of this line and the line is about to be executed again.
It is the combination of three icons which indicates that an error has occurred on the previous execution of this line and the line is about to be executed again. Also, the yellow icon indicates that a breakpoint is set for this line.
Related Links
  • Debugging in a Web Environment

    Debugging in a web environment requires special techniques because there is no Uniface client application to debug. For each HTTP request, Uniface starts an instance of the requested server page, and terminates it after the request is handled.

  • Debugging Remote and Server Processes

    To debug components that run in a different process than the client application, the Debugger and the application being debugged communicate with each other over a network. For this reason, it is often referred to as remote debugging, even if both the Debugger and application process run on the same local machine. The network protocol can be TCP or TLS.

Was this topic helpful?
LikeDislike
